questions.passage:
It is easy to mourn the picture palace. The chandeliers, the velvet, the usher with his flashlight; a thousand strangers inhaling at the same moment; the dark that made a crowd into an audience. Against that memory, the present looks like a fall: films arriving as files, watched alone on sofas, paused for the kettle, abandoned twenty minutes in. Something communal, we tell ourselves, has been lost — a great shared room that technology has emptied. I have written versions of that elegy myself. I no longer believe it, and the numbers are what changed my mind.

Begin with the era the elegy remembers. Weekly cinema attendance, in the countries where it was highest, reached its extraordinary peak in a window of roughly twenty-five years — from the coming of sound to the arrival of television. Before that window, moving pictures were one attraction among many, sharing bills with vaudeville acts. After it, attendance did not decline so much as collapse: in some countries it fell by three-quarters within fifteen years of television's spread, decades before anyone streamed anything. The universal moviegoing habit, the whole-family weekly ritual, turns out to have been not the natural state of the medium but a brief conjuncture — a generation's interlude between the music hall and the living room.

Now look harder at the room itself. The palaces of memory were flagships, a small fraction of screens, built as much to advertise the studios as to serve them; most cinemagoing happened in plain neighborhood houses with sticky floors and secondhand prints. And the reverent hush we project backward is largely a later invention. Contemporary accounts of the crowded decades describe halls full of talk, courtship, crying infants, and running commentary; patrons wandered in mid-film and stayed through the next showing until the story came round again. The silent, attentive auditorium — lights fully down, latecomers glared at — is substantially a norm of the art house and the post-collapse cinema, an etiquette that spread as audiences shrank. We remember a cathedral. The record shows a fairground that occasionally fell quiet.

None of this means nothing has been lost. Something has: the involuntary publicness of it, the impossibility of pausing, the knowledge that laughter was landing on every side of you. But loss is not the same as fall. What the elegy grieves is not the destruction of the medium's permanent home — the medium never had one. It grieves a particular arrangement of urban life, cheap land, scarce home entertainment, and young crowds with nowhere else to go, an arrangement that assembled itself for a few decades and then came apart, as arrangements do.

And here the elegist should sit with an uncomfortable thought. The fondest mourners of the packed Saturday house are, overwhelmingly, people who were young inside it. The palace they miss is lit by their own adolescence; the strangers laughing beside them were their own age once, and are not now. This does not embarrass the feeling — feelings need no license — but it should discipline the argument. A memory of one's youth is a poor measure of an institution's essence, and an essay that mistakes the first for the second will always find the present guilty.

The cinema is not dying; it is returning to something like its early scale, a special occasion rather than a habit, one screen among many. Whether that is worse depends on what one thought the habit was. If the packed house of midcentury was the medium's true form, we live among ruins. If it was a passing conjuncture — and the record says it was — then we live after an exception, which is where almost everyone has always lived.
questions.stem: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The decline of moviegoing was caused primarily by the rise of streaming services and could largely be reversed if exhibitors carefully restored the architectural splendor and communal conditions of the midcentury picture palace.
choices.B: Nothing of value has been lost in cinema's transformation, and those who mourn the shared auditorium are simply mistaken about their own feelings.
choices.C: Neighborhood cinemas, rather than ornate palaces, deserve recognition as the true architectural heritage of the moviegoing era.
choices.D: The universal moviegoing ritual mourned by elegists was a brief historical exception rather than the medium's natural state, so the present is better read as an aftermath of an anomaly than as a fall.

explanations.A: The passage dates the collapse to television's spread, 'decades before anyone streamed anything,' and proposes no restoration; the argument is that the peak was a conjuncture of conditions that came apart, not a state a business plan could revive.
explanations.B: The author explicitly grants that something real has been lost — the involuntary publicness, the laughter on every side — and says feelings 'need no license.' The correction aims at the argument built on the feeling, not at the feeling itself.
explanations.C: The neighborhood houses appear as evidence that the palaces were unrepresentative flagships, one step in the larger case; the passage advocates no heritage claim on their behalf.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage argues the weekly habit was 'a brief conjuncture,' the reverent auditorium a later norm 'projected backward,' and concludes that if the packed house was a passing exception, 'we live after an exception, which is where almost everyone has always lived' — loss without fall.

questions.stem: The opening paragraph's evocation of chandeliers, velvet, and a thousand strangers inhaling together primarily functions to:

choices.A: establish, in its full imaginative power, the elegiac picture that the rest of the passage will dismantle.
choices.B: provide the historical evidence on which the passage's argument about attendance patterns will rest.
choices.C: signal at the outset that the author regards all nostalgic feeling as contemptible and unworthy of serious analysis.
choices.D: argue that the sensory design of theaters mattered more to audiences than the films being shown in them.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph performs the elegy — the author admits having 'written versions of that elegy myself' — before announcing 'I no longer believe it.' Rendering the mourned picture vividly first gives the subsequent reversal its object and its force.
explanations.B: The paragraph is imagery and remembered feeling; the evidence arrives afterward, in the attendance figures and the contemporary accounts of noisy halls that the author says changed his mind.
explanations.C: The passage later insists the feeling is not embarrassed and 'needs no license'; the opening treats the elegy seriously enough to inhabit it, which is the opposite of contempt.
explanations.D: No comparison between architecture and films appears; the chandeliers and velvet belong to the mourner's memory being staged, not to a claim about what audiences valued most.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, the claim that the reverent hush is 'projected backward' most nearly means that:

choices.A: midcentury cinema audiences enforced silence so effectively that later generations could never hope to match their attentiveness.
choices.B: film historians have deliberately falsified contemporary accounts of how audiences behaved in crowded theaters.
choices.C: an etiquette that actually arose later, as audiences shrank, has been imagined onto an earlier era it does not describe.
choices.D: the design of the picture palaces acoustically amplified whatever quiet their audiences happened to produce.

explanations.A: This reverses the timeline the passage establishes: the crowded decades were 'full of talk, courtship, crying infants,' and the enforced hush belongs to the smaller audiences that came after.
explanations.B: The contemporary accounts are the author's evidence, presented as reliable; the projection is the work of collective memory and elegy, not of historians tampering with sources.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage states that the silent, attentive auditorium is 'substantially a norm of the art house and the post-collapse cinema, an etiquette that spread as audiences shrank' — a later convention retroactively attributed to the packed houses of the peak, a cathedral remembered where the record shows a fairground.
explanations.D: The phrase concerns the history of audience behavior and memory, not architecture or acoustics; no claim about sound design appears in the passage.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings, if true, would most weaken the passage's argument?

choices.A: Newly compiled attendance records show that weekly cinemagoing at near-peak levels persisted broadly for over a century, beginning well before sound and continuing long after television spread.
choices.B: Surveys show that people who attend the cinema today report enjoying their visits more than home viewers enjoy streaming.
choices.C: Architectural historians establish that the picture palaces were even more ornate than surviving photographs suggest.
choices.D: Extensive interviews conducted across several countries reveal that many older filmgoers freely acknowledge that their enduring fondness for the theaters of their childhood is closely tied to their own memories of youth.

explanations.A: Correct. The argument's foundation is that the universal habit occupied 'a window of roughly twenty-five years' — a brief conjuncture, not a natural state. A century of sustained near-peak attendance spanning both boundary events would destroy the exception claim and restore the elegists' picture of a long norm genuinely lost.
explanations.B: Present-day satisfaction comparisons say nothing about whether the midcentury habit was brief or enduring, which is the historical claim the argument rests on.
explanations.C: Greater ornateness would only sharpen the passage's point that the palaces were unrepresentative flagship advertisements; the argument does not depend on understating their splendor.
explanations.D: Mourners acknowledging the link to their youth confirms the passage's closing diagnosis rather than challenging it; the author already asserts that the palace 'is lit by their own adolescence.'

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the author's position on what the elegists have lost?

choices.A: Nothing real was lost, since the communal experience they describe never occurred in any theater.
choices.B: What was lost was primarily architectural, and could be recovered by preserving the surviving palaces.
choices.C: The loss is real but greatly exaggerated, since modern home viewing preserves most of what genuinely mattered about the shared auditorium.
choices.D: The loss is real, but its object is a temporary arrangement of social and economic conditions rather than the medium's permanent home.

explanations.A: The passage affirms the reality of the loss in so many words — 'None of this means nothing has been lost. Something has' — and names what: the involuntary publicness, the surrounding laughter. Denying the experience outright overshoots the author's correction.
explanations.B: The palaces are treated as unrepresentative flagships, and no preservation program is proposed; the mourned object is a social arrangement, not a building stock.
explanations.C: The author nowhere claims home viewing preserves the auditorium's virtues; the sofa, the pause, the solitary watching are presented as genuinely different, which is why something has in fact been lost.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage states that the elegy grieves 'a particular arrangement of urban life, cheap land, scarce home entertainment, and young crowds' that 'assembled itself for a few decades and then came apart' — a real loss whose object is a conjuncture, not the destruction of the medium's permanent home.

questions.stem: The passage's caution that 'a memory of one's youth is a poor measure of an institution's essence' would apply most directly to which of the following?

choices.A: A demographer who notes that most people form their strongest musical attachments in adolescence.
choices.B: A columnist who argues that newspapers were essentially a morning print ritual, based on the delivery rounds and breakfast tables of his boyhood.
choices.C: An economist who explains a downturn in restaurant visits by pointing to rising prices and changing work patterns.
choices.D: A retired athlete who freely admits that her sport probably seems far more graceful in her fond recollection than it ever appeared to spectators at the time.

explanations.A: The demographer is stating the general pattern about youthful attachment as a finding, not using her own youth to define what an institution essentially is; she is describing the trap, not falling into it.
explanations.B: Correct. The columnist converts the accidents of his own early experience — delivery rounds, the breakfast table — into the essence of an institution, exactly the mistake the passage names: the elegist's palace 'lit by their own adolescence' presented as the medium's true form.
explanations.C: The economist explains change through impersonal conditions rather than through remembered youth; nothing in the example involves mistaking autobiography for essence.
explanations.D: The athlete already applies the discipline the passage recommends: she recognizes that memory has flattered its object, rather than building an argument on the flattered version.
